question_title: Will the U.S. Congress pass (House and Senate) a standalone FAA extension or authorization bill before May 1, 2026?
summary: The FAA Reauthorization Act of 2024 (PL 118-63) already authorizes the FAA through FY2028, eliminating any legislative need for a new standalone extension or authorization bill. All aviation-related bills in the 119th Congress (Keep America Flying Act, Aviation Funding Stability Act, etc.) address other topics like shutdown protections, not FAA authorization. The only FAA funding action in 2026 was H.R. 7148, a broad government spending bill that explicitly fails the "standalone" criterion. With no qualifying bill introduced, no authorization gap to address, and only ~7 weeks remaining, this question almost certainly resolves NO.

factors.description: FAA already authorized through FY2028 via 2024 Reauthorization Act — no legislative need for a new standalone extension or authorization bill
No standalone FAA authorization/extension bill has been introduced in the 119th Congress — all aviation bills focus on shutdown protections or other topics
Only ~7 weeks remain until May 1, 2026 deadline — insufficient time to draft, introduce, committee-mark-up, and pass a standalone FAA bill through both chambers even if one were needed
FAA funding was addressed through a broader appropriations bill (H.R. 7148) in February 2026, which does not qualify as 'standalone'
Status quo persistence: Congress rarely passes standalone agency bills when no authorization gap exists and no crisis demands it

reasoning:
**Forecast: 3% probability (NO)**

The core reason is straightforward: the FAA is already authorized through FY2028 via the Reauthorization Act of 2024 (PL 118-63). There is no authorization gap to fill, so no standalone FAA extension/authorization bill is needed or in the pipeline.

Key evidence:
- **No need exists**: FAA authorization runs through FY2028
- **No qualifying bill exists**: All aviation bills in the 119th Congress address other topics (shutdown pay protections, innovation, etc.)
- **Recent FAA funding was non-standalone**: H.R. 7148 (Feb 2026) was a broad government spending bill covering "the vast majority of government agencies"
- **Insufficient time**: Only ~7 weeks remain, with no bill even introduced
- **No crisis trigger**: Nothing has emerged that would force emergency standalone FAA legislation

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if, before May 1, 2026, both the House and the Senate pass final version of a bill whose primary purpose is to extend or authorize the Federal Aviation Administration, and the bill is not packaged as part of a broader omnibus or continuing resolution covering multiple unrelated agencies. This question resolves to no otherwise. The primary source for bill actions is Congress.gov legislative status pages at https://www.congress.gov.
fine_print: “Standalone” means the enrolled measure’s scope is primarily FAA extension/authorization and is not a general government funding bill. If classification disputes arise, resolve using credible sources per https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions. Candidate Sources: GovTrack bill status (backup) https://www.govtrack.us/congress/bills | GovInfo enrolled bills collection (backup confirmation of passage) https://www.govinfo.gov/app/collection/bills
background_info:
This forecasts whether Congress resolves FAA authority/funding on a standalone basis, affecting aviation regulation, capacity, and industry planning.
